_description: Per-pattern explanations rendered in the comfort-posture dashboard. Each key is an exact pattern string emitted by apply-comfort-posture.py EMISSIONS or listed in dashboard-schema.json security_deny. Each value is { what, why }. Generated dashboards embed this file inline; the modal opened from each pattern's info button reads it.

Read(**).what: Read any file inside this project.
Read(**).why: The cheapest category of action — read-only on your local tree. Tightening this means Claude can't see your code without asking, which is rarely what you want.

Edit(**).what: Modify an existing file in this project.
Edit(**).why: Recoverable via git as long as you commit regularly. Setting this to autopilot is the 'let Claude refactor, I'll review the diff' workflow.

Write(**).what: Create a new file or overwrite an existing one in this project.
Write(**).why: Same recovery story as Edit — git tracks every change. Most users keep this at the same level as Edit.

MultiEdit(**).what: Apply multiple edits to one file in a single tool call.
MultiEdit(**).why: Functionally equivalent to Edit for permission purposes; bundled into one prompt instead of N prompts.

Read(~/**).what: Read any file in your home directory.
Read(~/**).why: Home often holds secrets — ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.netrc, browser profiles. Always-ask is the safe default even when you trust Claude.

Read(//**).what: Read any file at filesystem root (/etc/, /var/, /opt/, etc.).
Read(//**).why: System paths can leak host configuration. Always-ask unless you have a specific reason.

Edit(~/**).what: Modify a file in your home directory.
Edit(~/**).why: Touches ~/.gitconfig, ~/.zshrc, shell init files — changes propagate to every shell session. Usually a deliberate, one-off action.

Write(~/**).what: Create a file in your home directory.
Write(~/**).why: Same reasoning as Edit(~/) — writes outside the project skip git's safety net.

Edit(//**).what: Modify a file at filesystem root (e.g. /etc/hosts).
Edit(//**).why: Almost always requires sudo and almost always shouldn't happen from inside an agent loop.

Write(//**).what: Create a file at filesystem root.
Write(//**).why: Same reasoning as Edit(//) — system writes are infrequent enough that always-asking is fine.

Bash(ls:*).why: Read-only. The first command of nearly every exploration; the most common 'safe' shell action.

Bash(cat:*).why: Read-only. Used to read short files when Read tool isn't a fit (e.g. piping into another command).

Bash(head:*).what: Show the first N lines of a file.
Bash(head:*).why: Read-only. Quick peek at large files where Read would be overkill.

Bash(tail:*).what: Show the last N lines of a file (commonly used to follow logs).

Bash(wc:*).what: Count lines, words, or bytes in a file.
Bash(wc:*).why: Read-only. Used to size up files before deciding how to process them.

Bash(file:*).what: Identify a file's type by inspecting its contents.
Bash(file:*).why: Read-only. Useful when an extension is missing or wrong.

Bash(stat:*).what: Show file metadata (size, permissions, modified time).
Bash(stat:*).why: Read-only. Used to debug file-permission issues.

Bash(which:*).why: Read-only. 'Where does python3 actually live?' — debugging.

Bash(type:*).what: Show how a shell name is interpreted (alias, function, builtin, file).
Bash(type:*).why: Read-only. Used when a command behaves unexpectedly.

Bash(grep:*).why: Read-only. Used constantly during code exploration; restricting this makes Claude slow.

Bash(rg:*).what: Ripgrep — a faster, smarter grep used heavily by Claude.
Bash(rg:*).why: Read-only. Same role as grep but the default tool for codebase search.

Bash(find:*).what: Walk a directory tree by name, size, or time predicates.
Bash(find:*).why: Read-only when used without -delete or -exec rm. Used to discover files matching a pattern.

Bash(tree:*).what: Print a directory tree as ASCII art.
Bash(tree:*).why: Read-only. Used to give humans a quick visual of project layout.

Bash(echo:*).why: No side effects. Sometimes used for diagnostic output or composing pipelines.

Bash(git status:*).what: Show working-tree status — what's modified, staged, untracked.
Bash(git status:*).why: Read-only. The first thing to run before any git mutation.

Bash(git log:*).why: Read-only. Used to understand what changed when.

Bash(git diff:*).what: Show changes between working tree, index, or commits.

Bash(git show:*).what: Show details for a specific commit, tag, or object.
Bash(git show:*).why: Read-only. Used to inspect a single commit.

Bash(git branch:*).why: Read-only when used without -d/-D. The destructive variants are sub-categorized elsewhere.

Bash(git ls-files:*).why: Read-only. Quick way to enumerate the tracked set without walking the directory.

Bash(git remote:*).why: Read-only when used to list/show. (Adding/removing remotes is rare and worth pausing on.)

Bash(git blame:*).what: Show line-by-line authorship for a file.
Bash(git blame:*).why: Read-only. Used to understand who last touched a line and why.

Bash(git stash list).why: Read-only. Companion to git stash mutation operations.

Bash(gh pr view:*).why: Read-only. Used to load PR context before commenting or merging.

Bash(gh pr diff:*).why: Read-only. Reviewing a PR before opining.

Bash(gh pr checks:*).what: Show CI check status for a PR.
Bash(gh pr checks:*).why: Read-only. Used to see whether a PR is ready to merge.

Bash(gh issue view:*).why: Read-only. Used to load issue context.

Bash(gh run view:*).what: View a workflow run's status and logs.
Bash(gh run view:*).why: Read-only. Used to debug failed CI runs.

Bash(mkdir:*).why: Cheap and recoverable — rmdir undoes it. Used when scaffolding new code layout.

Bash(touch:*).what: Create an empty file or update its modified time.
Bash(touch:*).why: Cheap and recoverable. Used to scaffold a new file before writing content.

Bash(cp:*).why: The source file is untouched; the destination is a new file. Recoverable unless it overwrites something important.

Bash(mv:*).why: Renames are usually recoverable via git history. Tighten this if you're working in a directory where rename mistakes would be hard to spot.

Bash(ln:*).what: Create a hard or symbolic link.
Bash(ln:*).why: Cheap and recoverable. Used to create shortcuts or set up workspace layouts.

Bash(chmod:*).why: Used to make scripts executable. Recoverable, but bad chmod on a sensitive file can be subtle.

Bash(rm:*).why: Mostly recoverable from git history when the file was tracked. Override to always-ask if your workflow includes untracked files you'd be sad to lose. (rm -rf is hard-denied via security_deny.)

Bash(git add:*).what: Stage changes for the next commit.
Bash(git add:*).why: Trivially reversible via git restore --staged. Almost always safe.

Bash(git commit:*).what: Record staged changes as a commit.
Bash(git commit:*).why: Adds to local history. Reversible via git reset until pushed.

Bash(git checkout:*).what: Switch branches or restore files from a commit.
Bash(git checkout:*).why: Switching branches is safe when the working tree is clean. Checking out individual files overwrites them — git keeps the prior state in reflog.

Bash(git switch:*).what: Modern branch-switching command (the safer git checkout).
Bash(git switch:*).why: Designed specifically for branch operations; safer than checkout.

Bash(git stash:*).what: Save uncommitted changes for later (or apply previously stashed changes).
Bash(git stash:*).why: Stashes are recoverable via git stash list. Mostly used to swap between WIP states.

Bash(git restore:*).what: Restore working-tree or staged files from a commit.
Bash(git restore:*).why: Overwrites your current changes with the committed version. Mostly used to discard local edits.

Bash(git reset:*).why: Soft/mixed reset is safe and reversible. --hard is hard-denied via security_deny because it discards uncommitted changes irreversibly.

Bash(git merge:*).what: Merge a branch into the current branch.
Bash(git merge:*).why: Reversible via git reset until pushed. Merge conflicts surface for human resolution.

Bash(git rebase:*).what: Reapply commits on top of another base, rewriting history.
Bash(git rebase:*).why: Local history rewrites are reversible via reflog. Rebasing already-pushed branches is bad form (use a separate PR).

Bash(git tag:*).why: Local tags are reversible. Pushing tags is a separate action (git push --tags).

Bash(git push:*).what: Send commits to a remote (typically GitHub).
Bash(git push:*).why: Visible to teammates and triggers CI. Override to always-ask even when other remote-mutate operations are mostly-allowed, since pushes are the most consequential.

Bash(git fetch:*).what: Download refs from a remote without merging.
Bash(git fetch:*).why: Read-side of remote sync — doesn't modify your branches. Mostly safe.

Bash(git pull:*).what: Fetch and merge from a remote.
Bash(git pull:*).why: Combines git fetch + git merge. Can produce merge conflicts that pause for human input.

Bash(gh pr create:*).why: Visible to teammates and may trigger reviewer notifications. Typically worth confirming.

Bash(gh pr edit:*).what: Edit a PR's title, body, labels, or reviewers.
Bash(gh pr edit:*).why: Visible to everyone subscribed to the PR. Mostly recoverable but worth a pause.

Bash(gh pr comment:*).what: Post a comment on a PR.
Bash(gh pr comment:*).why: Visible to everyone subscribed. Comments can't be silently retracted (edits are timestamped).

Bash(gh pr merge:*).why: High-impact: merges into main, triggers deploys, closes the review. Almost always worth confirming.

Bash(gh pr close:*).why: Sends a 'closed' notification. Reopenable but the close is logged.

Bash(gh issue create:*).why: Visible to everyone in the repo. Lower-stakes than a PR but still public.

Bash(gh issue edit:*).what: Edit an issue's title, body, labels, or assignees.

Bash(gh issue comment:*).what: Post a comment on an issue.

Bash(npm publish:*).what: Publish a package to the npm registry.
Bash(npm publish:*).why: Hard-denied via security_deny. Once published, a version can't be replaced (only deprecated). Supply-chain risk is too high to allow from an agent loop.

Bash(pnpm publish:*).why: Same as npm publish — hard-denied via security_deny.

Bash(yarn publish:*).why: Same as npm publish — hard-denied via security_deny.

Bash(python:*).what: Run a Python script or python -c expression.
Bash(python:*).why: python -c can execute arbitrary code. Always-ask unless you're in a sandbox where exec is the whole point.

Bash(python3:*).why: Same as python — arbitrary code execution surface.

Bash(node:*).what: Run Node.js. With -e, executes arbitrary JavaScript.

Bash(bash -c:*).what: Run a string as a bash script.
Bash(bash -c:*).why: This is THE classic arbitrary-shell escape hatch. Always-ask.

Bash(eval:*).what: Evaluate a string as shell code in the current shell.
Bash(eval:*).why: Worse than bash -c — runs in your shell. Should almost always require confirmation.

Bash(ruby:*).what: Run a Ruby script or ruby -e expression.

Bash(perl:*).what: Run a Perl script or perl -e expression.

Bash(npm install:*).why: Many packages run post-install scripts with full shell access. Typosquats are real. Always-ask is the right default.

Bash(pnpm install:*).why: Same supply-chain concerns as npm install.

Bash(pip install:*).what: Install a Python package via pip.
Bash(pip install:*).why: Same supply-chain concerns — packages can run setup.py with shell access.

Bash(uv add:*).what: Add a dependency via uv (modern Python package manager).

Bash(uv pip install:*).why: Same as uv add / pip install.

Bash(brew install:*).why: Brew formulae are reviewed but a malicious or compromised tap can still ship trouble.

Bash(apt install:*).why: System-level install — touches /usr/, may write to /etc/, runs post-install scripts as root.

Bash(cargo install:*).why: Compiles arbitrary Rust code from crates.io. Same supply-chain concerns.

Bash(go install:*).why: Same supply-chain concerns. Go modules pin by version hash, which helps.

WebFetch.what: Read a URL via Claude Code's built-in fetcher.
WebFetch.why: Read-only HTTP. Used to look up docs, fetch reference pages.

Bash(curl:*).what: Make HTTP requests with curl (read by default; --request POST etc. are categorized as network_write).
Bash(curl:*).why: GETs without -X/--request are reads. Mostly safe; risk is what Claude does with the response.

Bash(wget:*).why: Always a read — writes the response to disk. Risk is similar to curl GET.

Bash(curl -X POST:*).what: Curl POST — sends data to a remote endpoint.
Bash(curl -X POST:*).why: Mutates remote state (charges API, posts message, triggers workflow). Always-ask unless you're in a sandbox.

Bash(curl -X PUT:*).what: Curl PUT — replaces a remote resource.

Bash(curl -X DELETE:*).what: Curl DELETE — removes a remote resource.

Bash(curl -X PATCH:*).what: Curl PATCH — partially updates a remote resource.

Bash(curl --request POST:*).what: Curl POST using the long-form --request flag.

Bash(gh api PATCH:*).what: Direct GitHub API PATCH call (used for fine-grained PR/issue/repo updates).
Bash(gh api PATCH:*).why: Mutates remote state on github.com. Recoverable but logged.

Bash(gh api DELETE:*).why: Mutates remote state, often irreversibly (e.g., deleting a release).

Bash(rm -rf:*).what: Recursive force-delete — removes a directory tree without confirmation.
Bash(rm -rf:*).why: Hard-denied. The classic disaster. Once 'rm -rf /important/path' runs there's no recovery short of backups. Lower-friction alternatives: rm individual files, git restore, trash-cli.

Bash(git push --force:*).why: Hard-denied. Destroys teammates' commits if they pushed in between. Use --force-with-lease (still confirm) when you genuinely need to rewrite a remote branch.

Bash(git reset --hard:*).what: Discard working-tree and staged changes irreversibly.
Bash(git reset --hard:*).why: Hard-denied. No undo — uncommitted work is gone. Use git stash or git restore for recoverable alternatives.

Bash(git clean -fd:*).why: Hard-denied. Deletes work git doesn't know about — including new files you haven't yet added. Use git clean -ni to preview first.

Bash(cargo publish:*).what: Publish a Rust crate to crates.io.
Bash(cargo publish:*).why: Hard-denied. crates.io versions are immutable once published. Same supply-chain reasoning as npm publish.

Bash(curl * | sh).what: Pipe downloaded content directly into a shell — the 'curl pipe sh' install pattern.
Bash(curl * | sh).why: Hard-denied. Runs arbitrary remote code with zero review. Use the package manager (brew/apt/pip) or download-then-inspect-then-run.

Read(.env).why: Hard-denied. .env typically holds API keys, DB passwords, and other secrets. If Claude needs an env var, read it via process.env at runtime, not by parsing the file.

Read(**/*.pem).what: Read a PEM-encoded private key (TLS certs, SSH keys).
Read(**/*.pem).why: Hard-denied. Private keys never need to be in an agent's working memory.

Read(**/credentials*).what: Read any file literally named 'credentials' (AWS, GCP, etc.).
Read(**/credentials*).why: Hard-denied. AWS ~/.aws/credentials and similar files hold long-lived access keys.

Read(**/secrets*).what: Read any file literally named 'secrets' (generic catch-all).
Read(**/secrets*).why: Hard-denied. Files literally named 'secrets' are almost always exactly that.
